ontology.foodex2_description: Flatbread — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 category for paratha; no dedicated paratha code exists in FoodEx2 release 2.0
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for tandoori paratha; A000E (flatbread) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tandoori paratha or paratha as a distinct food concept in the reviewed release.

serving.natural_language_portion: One tandoori paratha as typically served in a Singapore Indian restaurant weighs approximately 80 g, representing a single round layered flatbread with butter applied on top.

nutrient_source.title: Paratha, plain — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), Table 1: Cereal Products
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports plain paratha (whole wheat, tawa-cooked with ghee) per 100 g. Tandoori paratha is baked in a tandoor rather than on a tawa and is typically made with a mix of whole wheat and refined flour, with butter applied on top. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha as the closest approved primary source. Butter topping adds marginal fat; the core macro profile is consistent with ICMR-NIN data. USDA SR Legacy entry for 'Bread, whole-wheat, commercially prepared' (NDB 18069) was used as a cross-check for fibre and sodium. Nutrient values represent a reasonable central estimate for a butter-finished tandoori paratha.

gi_source.title: Chapati / Roti (whole wheat flatbread) — Glycemic Index Database, University of Sydney; supplemented by: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for tandoori paratha was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest approved analogous food is chapati / roti (whole wheat flatbread), which has a reported GI of approximately 62 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Tandoori paratha shares the same primary ingredient (wheat flour), similar preparation (dry heat baking), and comparable macronutrient profile. The addition of butter and layering technique may marginally slow gastric emptying, supporting a GI estimate at the lower end of the medium range. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (62 × 37.8 g) / 100 ≈ 23.4; rounded to 22.3 after adjusting for butter fat contribution reducing net available carbohydrate to approximately 36 g per 80 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and refined carbohydrate load
hypertension.guidance: Tandoori paratha contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one paratha and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or salted chutneys to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 600 mg per meal threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index and high carbohydrate density
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 62 (medium) and approximately 47 g carbohydrate per 100 g, tandoori paratha can cause a moderate postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to one paratha per meal, pair it with high-fibre vegetables or legume-based curries to lower the overall glycemic response, and monitor blood glucose accordingly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter or ghee finishing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The butter or ghee applied to tandoori paratha contributes approximately 4.8 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one paratha per sitting and request minimal butter topping to reduce saturated fat intake in line with the AHA recommendation of less than 13 g saturated fat per day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Paratha is a thin, round, layered Indian flatbread that is baked in a traditional clay oven called a tandoor, giving it a characteristic slight char and smoky aroma on the outer surface while remaining soft and flaky inside. It is made from a dough of whole wheat flour (atta) and/or refined wheat flour (maida), water, salt, and a small amount of fat such as ghee or oil, which is folded into the dough to create its distinctive layered structure. After baking, butter or ghee is applied on top, adding richness and a glossy finish. Tandoori paratha is a staple bread in North Indian cuisine and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, typically as an accompaniment to curries, dal, paneer dishes, or grilled meats. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy, carbohydrate-dense food providing approximately 326 kcal per 100 g, with 8.1 g protein, 11.5 g fat (including 4.8 g saturated fat from butter/ghee), and 47.2 g carbohydrate with 3.1 g dietary fibre. Its medium glycemic index (approximately 62) means it produces a moderate postprandial blood glucose rise, making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat content is clinically relevant for cardiovascular disease management, and the sodium level warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. It is suitable for vegetarians and is generally halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified butter or ghee.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tandoori paratha?
Is tandoori paratha suitable for diabetics?
What is the difference between paratha and naan?
What is the GI of paratha?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for tandoori paratha exists; values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha (whole wheat, ghee-cooked) as the closest approved primary source, with USDA SR Legacy used as a cross-check. GI is an analogous estimate based on chapati/roti from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the adaptation introduces uncertainty in fat content due to variable butter application.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN plain paratha; tandoori preparation and butter topping may increase fat and energy slightly compared to tawa-cooked paratha. 2. Vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; wheat-based flatbreads contain no meaningful vitamin C and ICMR-NIN reports 0 for this food category. 3. Vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; paratha is a plant-based bread product with no animal-derived B12 source in the dough itself (butter contributes negligible B12). 4. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 5. Selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 6. GI is an analogous estimate based on chapati/roti (GI ≈ 62) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for tandoori paratha was found. 7. FoodEx2 code A000E (flatbread) is used as the closest defensible category anchor; no dedicated FoodEx2 code for paratha exists. 8. Serving size of 80 g is based on typical restaurant portion for one paratha as served in Singapore Indian restaurants. 9. Sodium value of 310 mg per 100 g reflects salt added during dough preparation and is consistent with ICMR-NIN and USDA cross-check data.

Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases list.
